query: effectiveness of topical steroids in treating bullous lesions caused by HSV

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucosal Lesions, Immunologic Diseases -- Treatment / Management -- Erythema Multiforme
retrieved_docs.content: Erythema multiforme treatment primarily involves removing the inflicting agent and site-specific management. In the instance of medication-induced erythema multiforme, the causative drug should be discontinued. Likewise, infection-associated erythema multiforme is managed via treatment of the related infection. For acute erythema multiforme caused by an HSV infection, the use of antiviral therapy is controversial. The presentation of erythema multiforme symptoms follows the clinical presentation of HSV, potentially making antiviral therapy ineffective. Site-specific management for mild oral lesions involves topical corticosteroids (eg, 0.05% fluocinonide gel). For severe oral lesions, oral prednisone 40 to 60 mg/day tapered over 2 to 4 weeks may be used. Intravenous fluids may be necessary for those patients who become dehydrated due to painful oral lesions. Similar to mucous membrane pemphigoid, the presence of ocular lesions necessitates the involvement of the ophthalmology team. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Ophthalmicus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Stromal keratouveitis: Topical steroids either 1% prednisolone acetate or 0.1% dexamethasone resolves the underlying immune-mediated inflammation and prevents scarring. The topical antiviral cover is essential as HSV reactivation causes severe epithelial disease or stromal necrosis. Oral Acyclovir 400 mg twice daily for six months to one year prevents recurrences.

retrieved_docs.title: Keratitis -- Treatment / Management -- Viral Keratitis
retrieved_docs.content: Dual staining with Rose-Bengal and fluorescein stain is a very important clinical tool to make a diagnosis of HSV epithelial disease. Fluorescein stain makes the dendrites and geographical ulcers more evident by staining the base of the ulcer, and Rose-Bengal stains the cells at the margin of the ulcer, which are loaded with viruses. The diagnosis of epithelial disease is mostly clinical. [87] Corneal scraping for polymerase chain reaction for HSV viral DNA is done in doubtful cases. [88] Topical antiviral (acyclovir 3%- 5 times a day) is the mainstay of treatment for epithelial disease. [27] For HSV stromal disease and endotheliitis, a topical steroid is the mainstay of treatment. However, oral antiviral (acyclovir 400 mg, 5 times daily) is also added to prevent further recurrence. [89] For HSV necrotizing stromal keratitis, treatment has to be given at the earliest to avoid corneal melt and subsequent perforation. The loading dose of antiviral both topical acyclovir (3%)  and oral (acyclovir 400 mg 5 times daily ) is given for the initial three days. [31] Topical steroid is added on the third day. For cases presenting as severe thinning or perforation, tissue adhesive (cyanoacrylate glue) and bandage contact lens are applied. Preferably, a topical steroid is started after applying tissue adhesive to avoid steroid-induced further stromal lysis. For recurrent HSV keratitis, a prophylactic dose of oral antiviral (acyclovir 400 mg twice daily) is given for a year. [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Type 2 -- Treatment / Management -- Primary Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Primary herpes genitalis: 3 x 400 mg tablets PO daily for 7 to 10 days Severe primary herpes genitalis: 3 x 5 mg/kg IV daily for 5 to 7 days Recurrent herpes genitalis (less than 5 to 6 episodes/year): 400 mg PO twice a day for 3 days Prophylaxis: 2 x 400 mg PO daily for 6 months

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Treatment / Management -- Disciform Keratitis Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid treatment should be minimal when active epithelial disease is present. Treatment with antivirals should be intensified. Initially, the topical antiviral should be given 5 times daily with steroids 2 or 3 times daily, titrated based on the clinical assessment. Oral antiviral treatment may be helpful, but the efficacy of the oral treatment is not established yet. Oral steroids are used to reduce severe stromal inflammation as an adjunct therapy to reduce steroid-induced IOP elevation. The steroid also helps in reducing the replication of infectious viral keratitis. Topical cyclosporin 0.05% may help taper steroid treatment, particularly in the presence of epithelial disease, and prevent steroid-induced IOP elevation. Fine needle diathermy and laser techniques have also been reported to be beneficial in addressing corneal neovascularization and improving vision. [101]

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Ophthalmicus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dendritic ulcer: Most cases resolve spontaneously. Topical antivirals provide faster resolution in marginal ulcers, ulcers larger than 4 mm, and ulcers with stromal inflammation. Topical antivirals provide faster resolution with less scarring. Topical acyclovir 3% ointment or Ganciclovir 0.15% gel applied five times a day for two weeks will heal most of the ulcers. Surface debridement of the ulcer helps in reducing the viral load and offers better penetration of the medication. Toxicity is uncommon but presents as diffuse punctate erosions or follicular conjunctivitis. Most of the time, adding preservative-free lubricating eyedrops would be enough.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Topical aqueous suppressants: These agents are commonly used in combination with topical corticosteroids to treat elevated IOP secondary to HZO.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1309
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals, topical constitute a class of antiviral medications that are formulated for application to the skin or mucous membranes. Within this class, acyclovir 50 MG/ML Topical Cream is a specific preparation used to treat localized viral infections such as herpes simplex. Additionally, chemotherapeutics for topical use represent other agents that belong to the topical antiviral category, providing alternative or adjunctive options for managing surface‑level viral diseases. Together, these products illustrate the range of topical antiviral therapies available for targeted, localized treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1377
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidin is linked to a wide range of medicated shampoos that incorporate chlorhexidine gluconate as a primary active moiety, often combined with additional antifungal or keratolytic agents. Specific formulations in which chlorhexidin has the active moiety include “chlorhexidine gluconate 10 MG/ML / ketoconazole 2.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Ketodine],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [MiconaHex+Triz],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 30 MG/ML / climbazole 5 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” and “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Chlor].” In addition, chlorhexidin is identified as an ingredient in several broader shampoo products, namely “chlorhexidine / climbazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / miconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” and the simple “chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo.” Across all these products, chlorhexidine (often in the form of chlorhexidine gluconate) serves as a key antiseptic component, while the accompanying agents such as ketoconazole, miconazole nitrate, climbazole, and salicyloyl phytosphingosine provide antifungal or keratolytic effects, creating combination therapies aimed at treating scalp infections, dandruff, and related dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: Ketoconazole is a widely used antifungal agent incorporated in numerous medicated shampoos (e.g., Nizoral 10 mg/mL and 20 mg/mL, Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine, Ketochlor, Pharmaseb, and generic ketoconazole shampoo). In many formulations it is combined with the broad‑spectrum antiseptic chlorhexidine gluconate (10–40 mg/mL), the topical corticosteroid hydrocortisone (5 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, or hydrocortisone acetate 5 mg/mL), the keratolytic/skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL), chloroxylenol, or other antifungals such as miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) and climbazole (5 mg/mL). Representative combination products include chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz), chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL, chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL (with or without salicyloyl phytosphingosine), chloroxylenol / ketoconazole, chloroxylenol / miconazole nitrate / salicylic acid, and hydrocortisone‑only shampoos (5 mg/mL or acetate 5 mg/mL). Zinc pyrithione 19 mg/mL is also marketed as an antibacterial/anti‑seborrheic medicated shampoo. The “medicated shampoo” dosage form is a topical scalp preparation intended for fungal, bacterial, and seborrheic scalp disorders, delivering antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic actions. The extensive range of active ingredients and concentrations allows clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific microbial, fungal, inflammatory, or keratolytic needs of individual patients. From a safety standpoint, topical ketoconazole‑containing shampoos are contraindicated in patients receiving the systemic agents dihydroergotamine mesylate, dihydroergotamine tartrate, or ubrogepant due to potential adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.content: and antifungal‑only ketoconazole or climbazole formulations. Collectively, these formulations illustrate how Medicated Shampoos integrate antiseptics, antifungals, corticosteroids, keratolytic agents, and moisturizers in defined concentrations to provide targeted therapeutic effects for scalp and skin disorders. The variety of concentration levels and combination patterns enables clinicians to tailor therapy according to the severity of infection, presence of inflammation, need for keratolysis, or moisturizing support, thereby supporting patient‑centered management of dermatologic and scalp conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
retrieved_docs.content: Medicated Shampoo is a therapeutic topical preparation offered in numerous dose‑form variants that are defined by specific active‑ingredient combinations and concentrations. The primary antiseptic moiety, chlorhexidine (often as chlorhexidine gluconate), appears in a wide range of formulations, including chlorhexidine 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 23 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL, and 40 mg/mL. Chlorhexidine is combined with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL, marketed as Ketochlor), miconazole nitrate (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL, marketed as Malaseb or MiconaHex+Triz), and climbazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL). Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone is incorporated in multi‑agent products (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL). Keratolytic and barrier‑supporting agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) are added to several chlorhexidine‑based shampoos (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Stand‑alone chlorhexidine preparations (e.g., chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL, Vital‑Clor) are also listed. Additional medicated shampoos contain other active agents: miconazole alone or with salicylic acid (e.g., miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL), chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL combined with miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL and salicylic acid 20 mg/mL, urea 100 mg/mL, and zinc pyrithione 3 mg/mL (the latter includes the inactive ingredient alpha‑tocopherol). These diverse formulations deliver antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, keratolytic, and moisturizing effects, targeting scalp infections, fungal dermatoses, inflammatory conditions, hyperkeratosis, and general dermatologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

Dapsone is a member of the drug class used for the treatment of leprosy and is also employed in managing herpetic skin inflammation, illustrating its utility in both chronic bacterial infection and certain viral‑induced dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: and systemic use. Plain dermatological corticosteroid preparations constitute a subset of dermatological products and can be formulated across the full potency spectrum—from weak (group I) through moderately potent (group II) to very potent (group IV)—allowing therapy to be tailored to skin disease severity. The taxonomy further defines “corticosteroids, weak/moderately potent/potent, other combinations” as subtypes of the general “corticosteroids, other combinations” class. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is noted as a therapeutic option for patients in a vegetative state, reflecting its role in critical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1812
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations, indicating that they represent a highly potent subgroup of topical steroid formulations used in dermatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 674
retrieved_docs.content: formulations include chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo, which integrates an antiseptic with a skin‑conditioning lipid, and salicylic acid 18 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, providing a focused keratolytic action. Collectively, these diverse Medicated Shampoo dose forms illustrate a comprehensive approach to treating scalp infections, inflammation, and hyperkeratosis by tailoring active ingredient combinations and concentrations to specific therapeutic goals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class of topical antivirals encompasses agents such as idoxuridin and the formulation acyclovir 50 MG/ML topical cream. Idoxuridin is chemically derived from deoxyuridin, making deoxyuridin its parent compound and indicating a close structural relationship within nucleoside analogues used for skin viral infections. Both idoxuridin and acyclovir 50 MG/ML topical cream are members of the “Antivirals, topical” category, highlighting that they share a common mechanism of action as topical agents that inhibit viral replication. Recognizing that these specific drugs belong to the same topical antiviral class assists clinicians in selecting appropriate topical treatments for conditions such as herpes simplex virus lesions, taking into account factors like drug formulation, potency, and patient tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: are classified as plain dermatological preparations, representing distinct potency subclasses of simple topical steroids. Conversely, “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations”, “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”, and “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations” are subclasses of “corticosteroids, other combinations”, indicating formulation with additional agents rather than as stand‑alone preparations. The hierarchical organization of potency groups (weak, moderately potent, potent, very potent) and formulation types (plain vs. combination) clarifies how hydrokortison and related corticosteroids are positioned across therapeutic protocols, dermatologic products, and combination drug regimens, underscoring their central role in the management of inflammatory skin conditions and related disorders.
